The function $ S(t) = M e^{ -e^{-kt + C} } $ is a solution to the differential equation $ \frac{dS}{dt} = kS \ln\left(\frac{M}{S}\right) $, and we will now prove this.

Starting with the given function:
$$
S(t) = M e^{ -e^{-kt + C} }
$$
we can compute $ \frac{dS}{dt} $ by differentiating with respect to $ t $.

First, note that:
$$
\frac{d}{dt} \left( -e^{-kt + C} \right) = -k e^{-kt + C}
$$
Thus,
$$
\frac{dS}{dt} = M \cdot \frac{d}{dt} \left( e^{ -e^{-kt + C} } \right)
$$
$$
= M \cdot e^{ -e^{-kt + C} } \cdot \left( -k e^{-kt + C} \right)
$$
$$
= -k M e^{ -e^{-kt + C} } e^{-kt + C}
$$
$$
= -k M e^{ -e^{-kt + C} - kt + C }
$$

Now, let's compute the right-hand side of the differential equation:
$$
kS \ln\left(\frac{M}{S}\right)
$$
Substitute $ S(t) $:
$$
k M e^{ -e^{-kt + C} } \ln\left(\frac{M}{M e^{ -e^{-kt + C} }} \right)
$$
$$
= k M e^{ -e^{-kt + C} } \ln\left( e^{ e^{-kt + C} } \right)
$$
$$
= k M e^{ -e^{-kt + C} } \cdot e^{-kt + C}
$$
$$
= k M e^{ -e^{-kt + C} - kt + C }
$$

This matches the expression we derived for $ \frac{dS}{dt} $, so the function $ S(t) = M e^{ -e^{-kt + C} } $ is indeed a solution to the differential equation.
